request.question: Who wrote the debut song on the debut album of the artist who also released the album "I'm Still Here"?
request.answer: Billy Henderson and Steven Dale Jones

title: Neon Hitch discography
The Love Song (Jeff Bates song)
Crazy Over You
A Little More Country Than That
Mindy McCready
You Won't Ever Be Lonely (song)
Ten Thousand Angels (song)
Drunker Than Me
A Better Man
What I Really Meant to Say
sentences: The discography of British singer and songwriter Neon Hitch.
Neon's debut digital single "Get Over U" was released in February 2011.
Hitch then released her single "Bad Dog", which was intended to be the lead single from her debut album.
Later in 2011, she was featured on Gym Class Heroes' song "Ass Back Home".
In 2012, Neon Hitch released "Fuck U Betta" and Gold" featuring Tyga, her official first and second singles respectively.
Both songs peaked at number one on the "Billboard" Dance/Club Play chart.
Neon then debuted an EP entitled "Happy Neon" in January 2013, which was released online for free.
In October 2013, Neon announced that her debut album "Beg, Borrow & Steal" had been scrapped and she would release a new album that had more of her soul in it.
In January 2014, Neon released the mixtape "301 to Paradise" for free.

In May 2014, it was announced that Hitch had parted ways with her label Warner Bros. and was gearing up to releasing her new debut album "Eleutheromaniac"; she also released "Happy Neon" and "301 to Paradise" to digital retailers independently in the same month.
She premiered the lead single of "Eleutheromaniac", "Yard Sale", in August 2014.
In January 2015, Neon released "Sparks" as the first single from the album.
In March 2015, Hitch released the EP "24:00" for free.
In 2016, it was announced that Hitch had changed the name of her debut album to "Anarchy", which was released on July 22, 2016.
The lead single from the album, "Please", was released on July 8, 2016.
The album did not contain any of the singles intended to be on "Eluetheromaniac", but included a promomotional single she released in 2015, "Freedom".
"The Love Song" is the title of a debut song co-written and recorded by American country music Jeff Bates.
It was released in December 2002 as the lead-off single from his debut album "Rainbow Man", released on RCA Nashville.
The song was also Bates' highest-charting single on the Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart, reaching a peak of #8.
Bates wrote this song with Kenny Beard and Casey Beathard.
"Crazy Over You" is a debut song recorded by American country music duo Foster & Lloyd, who also wrote the song.
It was released in May 1987 as the first single from their self-titled debut album.
It was their most successful single, peaking at #4 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs chart in 1987.
The song was simultaneously recorded and released by Ricky Van Shelton on his debut album, "Wild-Eyed Dream".
"A Little More Country Than That" is a debut song written by Joey + Rory's Rory Lee Feek, Wynn Varble and Don Poythress, and recorded by American country music artist Easton Corbin.
It was released in August 2009 as the first single from his self-titled debut album.
In April 2010, the song reached Number One on the country music charts, making Corbin the first male solo artist in seven years to send a debut single to Number One.
It has been certified Gold by the RIAA.
Malinda Gayle "Mindy" McCready (November 30, 1975 – February 17, 2013) was an American country music singer.
Active from 1995 until her death in 2013, she recorded a total of five studio albums.
Her debut album, 1996's "Ten Thousand Angels", was released on BNA Records and was certified 2× Platinum by the RIAA, while 1997's "If I Don't Stay the Night" was certified Gold.
1999's "I'm Not So Tough", her final album for BNA, was less successful, and she left the label.
A self-titled fourth album followed in 2002 on Capitol Records.
McCready's fifth and final studio album, "I'm Still Here", was released in March 2010 on Iconic Records.
"You Won't Ever Be Lonely" is a debut song co-written and recorded by American country music singer Andy Griggs.
It was released in November 1998 as his debut single, and was served as the lead-off single and title track from his debut album "You Won't Ever Be Lonely".
It peaked at number 2 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks charts.
Griggs wrote the song with Brett Jones.
"Ten Thousand Angels" is a debut song written by Billy Henderson and Steven Dale Jones, and recorded by American country music artist Mindy McCready.
It was released on April 1, 1996 as the first single and title track from the album "Ten Thousand Angels".
The song reached #6 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart.
"Drunker Than Me" is the title of a debut song co-written and recorded by American country music artist Trent Tomlinson.
It was released in October 2005 as the first single from his debut album "Country Is My Rock".
Tomlinson wrote the song with Ashe Underwood.
"A Better Man" is title of a debut song co-written and performed by American country music artist Clint Black.
It was released in February 1989 as his debut single, and was served as the first single from his debut album, "Killin' Time".
It was written by Black and Hayden Nicholas.

When "A Better Man" went to No. 1 on the "Billboard magazine" Hot Country Singles chart on June 10, Black was the first artist since Freddy Fender to ascend to the top of the country chart with his first charted single.
In addition, "A Better Man" was the No. 1 song of 1989 on the Hot Country Singles chart.
The success of "A Better Man," along with the follow-up "Killin' Time," were instrumental in Black winning the Country Music Association's Horizon Award in 1989.
"What I Really Meant to Say" is a debut song written by Cyndi Thomson, Tommy Lee James and Chris Waters.
It was released in March 2001 as Thomson's debut single, as well as the lead-off single from her debut album "My World".
The song reached the top of the Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart, giving Thomson her only Number One single on that chart.
It also made her the third country music artist that year to reach Number One with a debut single.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.question: Who wrote the debut song on the debut album of the artist who also released the album "I'm Still Here"?
response.gold_answer: Billy Henderson and Steven Dale Jones

response.hop1_query: Who wrote the debut song on the debut album of the artist who also released the album "I'm Still Here"?
hop1_docs: Cold (But I'm Still Here) | "Cold (But I'm Still Here)" is the first single off Evans Blue's debut album, "The Melody and the Energetic Nature of Volume".  The song was released December 13, 2005, two months prior to the album release, and garnered frequent radio play in anticipation of the upcoming album.  Evans Blue's official MySpace explains the meaning of this song as well as others by the group.  A music video was also produced for "Cold (But I'm Still Here)."  The song currently has over three million plays on MySpace.

Kick Back Relax | "Kick Back Relax" is a pop song recorded by Swedish singer and Idol winner Agnes taken from her second album "Stronger".  The track was written by Jörgen Elofsson who also wrote her debut single "Right Here Right Now".  It was released as the album's first single in September 2006.

Crazy Over You | "Crazy Over You" is a debut song recorded by American country music duo Foster & Lloyd, who also wrote the song.  It was released in May 1987 as the first single from their self-titled debut album.  It was their most successful single, peaking at #4 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs chart in 1987.  The song was simultaneously recorded and released by Ricky Van Shelton on his debut album, "Wild-Eyed Dream".

Outta Here (song) | "Outta Here" is the title song of the debut studio album by Dutch R&B singer-songwriter Esmée Denters.  It was released as the debut single both from the artist and the album in the Netherlands on 14 April 2009, 27 April in New Zealand followed by 16 August in the UK.

Gabriela (singer) | Her self-titled debut album was released in 2001; it was produced by Vehnee Saturno, who also wrote eight of the album's ten songs.  Another of the songs was a rendition of Bryan Adams's "Please Forgive Me".  Her second album, "In the Interim", was released in 2004.

Anywhere but Here (song) | "Anywhere but Here" is the title of the fourth single released by American country music artist Sammy Kershaw.  It was co-written by Bob DiPiero and John Scott Sherrill with Buddy Cannon, who also produced it.  It was released in September 1992 as the final single from his debut album, "Don't Go Near the Water".  It peaked at #10 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs chart and #17 on the Canadian "RPM" country singles chart.

Dollhouse (Melanie Martinez song) | "Dollhouse" is the debut single by American recording artist Melanie Martinez.  The song was featured on Martinez's debut EP "Dollhouse" (2014), where it was released as the EP's lead single on February 9, 2014.  "Dollhouse" was later featured on Martinez's debut studio album, "Cry Baby" (2015).  Lyrically, "Dollhouse" is about a dysfunctional family, who, according to Martinez, is "hiding being a perfect plastic facade".  Martinez has also stated that "Dollhouse" is a metaphor for how people view celebrities and their seemingly perfect public lives.  Dollhouse is also the second song of Cry Baby, and this song is also the first official song Melanie Martinez wrote.
response.summary_1: Passages do not identify any artist who released an album titled "I'm Still Here" (bridge missing). Provided evidence anchors for the next hop: "'Cold (But I'm Still Here)' is the first single off Evans Blue's debut album, 'The Melody and the Energetic Nature of Volume'"; "'Dollhouse' ... this song is also the first official song Melanie Martinez wrote"; "'Crazy Over You' ... a debut song ... Foster & Lloyd, who also wrote the song"; "'Kick Back Relax' ... written by Jörgen Elofsson who also wrote her debut single 'Right Here Right Now'"; and "'Outta Here' is the title song of the debut studio album by Dutch R&B singer-songwriter Esmée Denters." Next retrieval should (1) find the artist who released the album "I'm Still Here" and then (2) fetch that artist's debut album and the songwriting credit for the debut song.
response.hop2_query: I'm Still Here album artist genre
hop2_docs: I'm Still Here (album) | I'm Still Here is the fifth and final studio album by American country music artist Mindy McCready.  It was released on March 23, 2010, via Linus Entertainment.  The album includes the single "I'm Still Here," as well as re-recordings of her 1996 singles "Guys Do It All the Time" and "Ten Thousand Angels."  Mindy performed on "Fox & Friends" to promote the album in April 2010.  This is McCready's final release before her death in 2013.

You're Still Here | "You're Still Here" is a song recorded by American country music artist Faith Hill.  It was released in June 2003 as the fourth single from her fifth album "Cry" (2002).  The song reached #28 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart.  The song was written by Matraca Berg and Aimee Mayo.

Still Standing (Monica album) | Still Standing is the sixth studio album by American R&B recording artist Monica.  It was released on March 19, 2010 by J Records.  Recording for the album began in 2007, in which the singer intended to take a more traditional approach to the genre and take away any "gimmicks."  As executive producer of the album Monica enlisted a variety of producers including involvement by Missy Elliott, Bryan-Michael Cox, Stargate, and Jermaine Dupri.  Production on "Still Standing" was chronicled by Monica's which was aired between October 2009 and January 2010.
I'm Still Here... Damn It! | I'm Still Here... Damn It is an album released by singer/comedian/actress Sandra Bernhard.  The album is a live recording of her one-woman show of the same title;

Cold (But I'm Still Here) | "Cold (But I'm Still Here)" is the first single off Evans Blue's debut album, "The Melody and the Energetic Nature of Volume".  The song was released December 13, 2005, two months prior to the album release, and garnered frequent radio play in anticipation of the upcoming album.  Evans Blue's official MySpace explains the meaning of this song as well as others by the group.  A music video was also produced for "Cold (But I'm Still Here)."  The song currently has over three million plays on MySpace.

Amok (Sentenced album) | Amok is the third album by the Finnish metal band Sentenced.  The album was released through Century Media Records in 1995.  Though still mostly rooted in the death metal genre, this album takes the melodic death metal direction of 1993's "North from Here" a step further with elements of traditional heavy metal and doom metal, along with elements of the goth rock-inspired style that the band would take on with subsequent releases, following bassist/vocalist Taneli Jarva's departure.

Idol Tryouts | Idol Tryouts is the second various artists compilation album released by Michigan-based label Ghostly International.  Unlike the label's previous sampler "Disco Nouveau", "Idol Tryouts" contains music solely from artists signed to Ghostly International.  Musical genres represented here span techno, electro, electroclash and tech house.
response.summary_2: The passages contradict the context's "bridge missing" claim: passage [1] identifies Mindy McCready as the artist who released I'm Still Here (it is described as her fifth and final studio album). None of the provided passages state who wrote the debut song on Mindy McCready's debut album.
